text: The surgeon performs a roboticohepaticojejunostomy, beginning with the removal of adhesions. The ligament of Treitz is identified, and the jejunum is divided 20 centimeters below this point. The proximal jejunum is then brought up through the transverse mesocolon, and a jejunostomy is created using a robotic stapler.
The surgeon closes the common enterotomy using a 4-0 V-Loc suture on a CV-23 needle, applying a continuous suturing technique.
The surgeon performs hilar dissection, mobilizing the duodenum which was adhered to surrounding structures, and lowers the hilar plate.
The surgeon identifies and mobilizes the gallbladder, achieving the critical view of safety before performing a cholecystectomy.
An intraoperative ultrasound is used to verify the placement of a stent in the common bile duct. The duct is opened, revealing the bifurcation and the visible PTC stent.

The surgeon elevates the jejunum to the bifurcation of the common bile duct and creates a matching enterotomy. The duct opening measures approximately two centimeters, while a slightly smaller incision is made in the jejunum.

The surgeon constructs a diamond-shaped anastomosis starting at the six o'clock position in the duct, stitching upwards from both sides using a suture. The procedure involves using a 4-0 V-lock suture, with alternatives for smaller ducts including a 5-0 monocryl or a 6-0 suture with a TF needle.
The resident ties the suture at the overlap site, and a Weck clip is placed to secure the area.
The surgeon performs gastric suspension, creating a serosal patch to cover the anastomosis site.
